title: Unit C-2 Self-Check (3 questions, for confirming understanding -- no pass/fail record is saved)

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the relationship between generative AI and copyright?
choices: As long as something was generated by AI, it does not infringe copyright even if it closely resembles an existing work

Because generated output may closely resemble an existing copyrighted work, checking is needed before publishing or distributing it, and entering someone else's copyrighted work to create material for distribution can also raise issues of reproduction or adaptation
Copyright concerns only apply to for-profit companies and do not need to be considered when used at a hospital or university
Every generative AI service has identical terms of use, so once you have read them there is no need to check again
questions.explanation: Because generative AI output can include material, derived from its training data, that closely resembles existing copyrighted works, checking is needed before publishing or distributing it. In addition, entering someone else's copyrighted work, such as a textbook or paper, to produce material for distribution can also raise issues of reproduction or adaptation. The idea that AI-generated content never infringes copyright (option 1) is incorrect, and use at a hospital or university is not exempt from copyright considerations either (option 3). Terms of use differ across services and are also subject to revision (option 4).

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the relationship between generative AI output and bias?
choices: Proceed on the assumption that output always contains some bias originating from the training data, and correct for it when you notice it -- for example, by explicitly specifying conditions in the prompt
Using a paid version of the AI guarantees bias-free output
Bias does not occur for questions specifically in the medical field
If you are concerned about bias, you can feel reassured by asking the same AI, "Is your answer biased?" and receiving the answer, "No, it is not"
questions.explanation: Because generative AI output reflects statistical patterns in its training data, it must be handled on the assumption that it always contains some bias -- involving language, region, gender, era, and so on. Bias you notice can be corrected, for example, by explicitly specifying conditions in the prompt. Using a paid version does not guarantee the absence of bias (option 2), and the medical field is no exception (option 3). Asking the AI itself is not a guarantee, since that answer is generated by the same underlying mechanism (option 4).

questions.q: Which of the following best describes verification before using generative AI output in clinical work?
choices: If the output is fluent and specific, verification can be skipped
It is sufficient to have the AI itself perform the verification; human checking is not necessary
Human verification is mandatory before using output in clinical work, checked from three perspectives: fact-checking, fit for the intended audience, and harm potential
Once an AI service's output has passed verification once, all of its future output can be used without further verification
questions.explanation: Human verification is mandatory before using generative AI output in clinical work. The three perspectives are: (1) fact-checking -- cross-referencing with primary sources such as the package insert or guidelines; (2) fit for the intended audience -- whether it suits the intended reader or the conditions at your own institution; and (3) harm potential -- whether there is anything that could cause misunderstanding or give inappropriate advice. Fluency or specificity is not evidence of correctness (option 1), and having the AI check itself is not a substitute for human verification (option 2). Verification is needed for each individual output; treating a service as having passed once and for all is dangerous (option 4).
